description: Having one thing means having it — or something else entirely.

guides.text: The goal is $A \to (A \lor B)$. Read it aloud: if $A$ is true, then $A$ or $B$ is true. This is inescapable — adding an alternative can only make a statement **weaker**, never stronger.
An [implication|implication] goal calls for our standard opening: assume the left-hand side.

We have $A$ at step 1. The rule `or-intro-l` takes a truth and slots it onto the **left** side of an $\lor$. The right side can be anything you like — here, $B$. This captures the idea: '$A$ is true, so $A$ or $B$ is certainly true.'
Now discard the [assumption|assumption]. We proved $A \lor B$ by relying on $A$ — so $A \to (A \lor B)$ stands on its own.

unlocks.name: ∨-intro-l
unlocks.description: If you've proved $p$, you've proved $p \lor q$ — because the left side is already true. The $q$ can be anything.

unlocks.name: ∨-intro-r
unlocks.description: If you've proved $q$, you've proved $p \lor q$ — because the right side is already true. The $p$ can be anything.
